q: Lipids are highly soluble in water, making their transport in the aqueous plasma easy.
e: Lipids are insoluble in water, the problem of transportation in the aqueous plasma is solved by associating nonpolar lipids with amphipathic lipids.

q: Lipoproteins are spherical macromolecular complexes of lipids and specific proteins.
e: Lipoproteins are spherical macromolecular complex of lipid and specific proteins.

q: The nonpolar core of a lipoprotein consists mainly of amphipathic phospholipids and cholesterol molecules.
e: The nonpolar lipid core consists of mainly triacylglycerol and cholesteryl ester.

q: The surface layer of a lipoprotein contains polar groups that face outward to the aqueous medium.
e: Surrounded by a single surface layer of amphipathic phospholipid... oriented so that their polar groups face outward to the aqueous Medium.

q: The protein moiety of a lipoprotein is known as an apolipoprotein or apoprotein.
e: The protein moiety of a lipoprotein is known as an apolipoprotein or apoprotein.

q: There are exactly two main types of lipoproteins: HDL and LDL.
e: The four main types of lipoproteins are chylomicrons, VLDL, low-density lipoprotein (LDL), and high density lipoprotein (HDL).

q: Lipoproteins are classified based on their lipid and protein composition, density, size, and site of origin.
e: Lipoproteins are differing in lipid and protein, density, size and site of origin.

q: Lipoproteins become smaller and denser when the fat to protein ratio is increased.
e: Lipoproteins are larger and less dense when the fat to protein ratio is increased.

q: Chylomicrons are the largest lipoproteins in size and have the lowest density.
e: Chylomicrons are the largest in size and have low density (large amount of lipid).

q: Chylomicrons primarily carry newly synthesized triglycerides from the liver to the adipose tissue.
e: Chylomicrons... carry triglycerides (dietary fat) from the intestine to the liver and adipose tissue.

q: Very low density lipoproteins (VLDL) carry newly synthesized triglycerides from the liver to the adipose tissue.
e: Very low density lipoproteins (VLDL) carry (newly synthesized) triglycerides from the liver to the adipose tissue.

q: Intermediate-density lipoproteins (IDL) are derived from the catabolism of VLDL.
e: Intermediate- density lipoproteins (IDL) are derived from the catabolism of VLDL.

q: IDL particles are typically highly detectable in the blood of healthy individuals.
e: They are not usually detectable in the blood.

q: LDL carries cholesterol from the liver to the cells of the body.
e: Low density lipoproteins (LDL) carry cholesterol from the liver to cells of the body.

q: LDL is commonly referred to as the "good cholesterol" lipoprotein.
e: LDL is sometimes referred as the bad cholesterol lipoprotein.

q: High density lipoproteins (HDL) are the smallest in size and have a high density due to a large amount of protein.
e: High density lipoproteins (HDL) are the smallest in size and has high density (large amount of protein).

q: HDL collects cholesterol from the body's tissues and brings it back to the liver.
e: They collect cholesterol from the body's tissues, and bring it back to the liver.

q: Increased levels of plasma lipoproteins are clinically termed hypolipoproteinemia.
e: Increased or decreased plasma lipoproteins are named hyperlipoproteinemia & hypolipoproteinemia respectively.

q: A standard lipoprotein profile measures the total amount of cholesterol, HDL, LDL, and triglycerides in the plasma.
e: The lipoprotein profile measures: The total amount of cholesterol... levels of HDL... levels of LDL... amount of triglyceride.

q: HDL is composed of roughly 50% protein, 25% phospholipid, 20% cholesterol, and 5% triglycerides.
e: HDL is composed of 50% protein, 25% phospholipid, 20% cholesterol, and 5% triglycerides.

q: HDL removes excess cholesterol from tissues, effectively helping to clean the blood.
e: It removes excess cholesterol from tissues (it cleans blood).

q: A high level of HDL-C significantly increases the risk of heart and blood vessel disease.
e: High level of HDL-C is the better because it reduces the risk of heart and blood vessel disease.

q: Sample collection for a lipid profile typically requires a fasting period of 12 hours.
e: Lipid profile involves the measurement of total cholesterol, triglycerides, HDL and LDL after 12 hours fast.

q: Chemical precipitation cannot be used to separate lipoprotein types.
e: There are several techniques for separation... such as Ultracentrifugation, Electrophoresis, or chemical precipitation.

q: In the precipitation method for HDL-C estimation, HDL is precipitated out of the solution.
e: HDL is left in the supernatant solution for cholesterol quantitation.

q: Phosphotungstic acid (PTA) and magnesium chloride are used to precipitate Chylomicrons, LDL, and VLDL.
e: ...after precipitation of Chylomicrons, LDL and VLDL lipoproteins by phosphotungstic acid (PTA) and magnesium chloride.

q: LDL contains less protein content and more cholesterol compared to other lipoproteins like HDL.
e: It has less protein content and contains more cholesterol.

q: Reducing HDL levels is a major treatment target for cholesterol-lowering medications.
e: Reducing LDL levels is a major treatment target for cholesterol-lowering medications.

q: High levels of LDL in the blood can deposit in artery walls and trigger clot formation.
e: Because high LDL in blood will deposited in blood artery and trigger clot formation.

q: A desirable HDL level is considered to be greater than 60 mg/dl.
e: Desirable HDL > 60 mg/dl.

q: An HDL level of less than 40 mg/dl is considered low.
e: Low HDL < 40 mg/dl.

q: A desirable LDL level is strictly greater than 130 mg/dl.
e: Desirable LDL < 110 mg/dl.

q: An LDL level above 130 mg/dl is classified as high LDL.
e: High LDL > 130 mg/dl.

q: The Friedewald formula is routinely used in biochemical laboratories to calculate the concentration of LDL.
e: The routine method used in biochemical laboratory is calculation by Friedwald formula... LDL =

q: According to the Friedewald formula, VLDL is estimated by dividing the triglyceride (TG) level by 10.
e: VLDL is estimated as TG level divided by 5.

q: Total cholesterol is mathematically considered the sum of HDL, LDL, and VLDL.
e: Cho. = HDL + LDL + VLDL.

q: According to the Friedewald formula, LDL equals Total Cholesterol minus the sum of HDL and VLDL.
e: LDL = Cho. - (HDL + VLDL).

q: The nonpolar lipid core of a lipoprotein consists exclusively of free fatty acids.
e: The nonpolar lipid core consists of mainly triacylglycerol and cholesteryl ester.

q: Chylomicrons have a density of less than 0.95 g/ml.
e: Chylomicrons density <0.95.

q: HDL particles have the lowest density among all lipoproteins.
e: HDL has high density (large amount of protein) / Density 1.063-1.21 g/ml.

q: VLDL stands for Very Low Density Lipoprotein.
e: Very low density lipoproteins (VLDL).

q: HDL carries cholesterol from the liver to the body's organs.
e: HDL: good cholesterol, carry cholesterol from body cells to the liver. LDL carries it from liver to organs.

q: Excess cholesterol collected by HDL from tissues is eventually catabolized or excreted in the bile by the liver.
e: carry cholesterol from body cells to the liver where it catabolizes or excreted in the bile.

q: Lipids absorbed from the diet do not require specialized transport mechanisms because they freely dissolve in plasma.
e: Lipids are insoluble in water, the problem of transportation in the aqueous plasma is solved by associating... to make water-miscible lipoproteins.

q: VLDL has a larger particle diameter than HDL.
e: VLDL particle diameter is larger (approx 40-80 nm) compared to HDL (approx 5-20 nm) as shown on the chart.

q: Apoproteins are specific carbohydrate molecules attached to the surface of lipoproteins.
e: The protein moiety of a lipoprotein is known as an apolipoprotein or apoprotein.

q: IDL particles have a density intermediate between very low density and low density lipoproteins.
e: ...with a density ranging intermediate between very low density and Low density lipoproteins.

q: Phospholipids are nonpolar lipids found strictly within the core of the lipoprotein.
e: ...amphipathic lipids (phospholipids and cholesterol)... single surface layer of amphipathic phospholipid.

q: The density of LDL ranges between 1.019 and 1.063 g/ml.
e: The chart shows LDL density at 1.019-1.063 g/ml.

q: Lipids are essential components that require association with amphipathic molecules to become water-miscible for transport.
e: solved by associating nonpolar lipids... with amphipathic lipids... and proteins to make water-miscible lipoproteins.
